name: Mountain biking through the Sierra de Gredos
name_original: Circular BTT por la Sierra de Gredos
description:
The Sierra de Gredos, located in the Central System of Spain, is a natural paradise full of majestic peaks, impressive gorges and beautiful lagoons. Its mountainous landscapes and rich biodiversity offer a unique experience for your next adventure. Here, nature is shown in all its splendor and goats, foxes or deer are likely to cross your path.

Arenas de San Pedro is the starting point for the circular route. You can get there by car on the AV-923 highway that borders the mountain range on its southern slope. Here, mountains of more than 2000 meters are awaiting your arrival. Especially in spring and autumn, the contrast of colors in the vegetation is really beautiful. Of course, we recommend that you check the weather forecast to decide your equipment appropriately.

The itinerary is an interesting combo of tracks and paths, with some short stretches of road and demanding slopes that take you into the most absolute beauty of the mountains. In addition, you have the opportunity to visit different towns nestled in the mountains with rich cuisine and where you can still see the typical architecture of the area.
region_intro: Mountain biking around Arenas De San Pedro offers diverse terrain within the Sierra de Gredos mountain range and the Tiétar Valley. The region is characterized by immense granitic massifs, dense forests of chestnut, pine, and oak, and river valleys with olive groves. This varied landscape provides a network of mountain bike trails suitable for different skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Arenas De San Pedro The most popular mountain bike trail is Mountain biking through the Sierra de Gredos, a 25.6 miles (41.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 47 minutes to complete. This route traverses the rugged granitic massifs and high peaks of the Sierra de Gredos. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Arenas, El Hornillo y Guisando — Sierra de Gredos, a difficult 24.2 miles (39.0 km) path. It offers a challenging ride through varied landscapes including forest paths and rocky terrains. Local mountain bikers also love the Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge – Arbillas Viewpoint loop from Arenas de San Pedro, a 38.5 miles (61.9 km) trail leading through the Tiétar Valley and past historical landmarks, often completed in about 5 hours 42 minutes. Mountain biking around Arenas De San Pedro is defined by granitic massifs, dense forests, and river valleys. The extensive network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Arenas De San Pedro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 700 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Arenas De San Pedro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of mountain bike trails can I expect in Arenas de San Pedro?
A: The region offers a diverse network of mountain bike trails, ranging from moderate to difficult. You'll find singletrack, doubletrack, and mixed trails with features like jumps, rock faces, and rock gardens, particularly in the Sierra de Gredos. The Tiétar Valley provides a contrast with more fertile valleys and olive groves.

Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Arenas de San Pedro?
A: There are over 35 mountain bike trails around Arenas de San Pedro, catering to various skill levels. This includes 1 easy route, 18 moderate routes, and 18 difficult routes, ensuring options for every rider.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Arenas de San Pedro?
A: The mild climate in the valleys during spring and autumn makes these seasons ideal for exploring, offering spectacular natural colors. While summer can be hot, the higher elevations of the Sierra de Gredos provide cooler options. Winter riding is possible, but be mindful of potential snow in the higher mountains.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails in the area?
A: Yes, while many routes are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy mountain bike route available. For families, consider exploring the less technical paths in the Tiétar Valley, which offer gentler terrain and scenic views without extreme challenges.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails around Arenas de San Pedro?
A: The Sierra de Gredos and Tiétar Valley are generally dog-friendly, but specific trail rules may vary. It's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or livestock, and to ensure they are well-behaved and don't disturb other trail users or the natural environment.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural attractions can I see along the mountain bike routes?
A: Many routes pass by significant landmarks. You can ride past the 15th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6910756" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, a historical landmark over the Arenal river. The region also features impressive natural formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4618414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Almanzor&lt;/a&gt;, the highest peak in Gredos, and numerous natural pools formed by rivers like the Arenal, perfect for a refreshing dip.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes available?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6910756" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge – Arbillas Viewpoint loop from Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option, covering 38.5 miles (61.9 km) and offering diverse scenery. Another challenging loop is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6910775" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Pedro Bernardo – Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge loop from Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while mountain biking in Arenas de San Pedro?
A: The diverse landscapes of the Sierra de Gredos and Tiétar Valley are home to various wildlife. You might spot Spanish goats (ibex) in the mountains, as well as black storks, vultures, and eagles soaring overhead, adding to the natural allure of your ride.

Q: Do I need any permits for mountain biking in the Sierra de Gredos area?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for mountain biking on established trails in the Sierra de Gredos. However, it's always advisable to respect local regulations, especially within protected natural parks, and to stay on marked paths to minimize environmental impact.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about the trails in Arenas de San Pedro?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 10 reviews. Mountain bikers often praise the challenging terrain, the stunning views of the Sierra de Gredos, and the variety of trails that cater to different skill levels, from dense forests to rocky paths.

Q: Are there options for advanced mountain bikers looking for a challenge?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its challenging routes, with 18 difficult trails available. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1379081149/arenas-el-hornillo-y-guisando-sierra-de-gredos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arenas, El Hornillo y Guisando — Sierra de Gredos&lt;/a&gt; offer demanding climbs and technical descents through varied landscapes, including forest paths and rocky terrains, providing a thrilling experience for experienced riders.

Q: Is there parking available at the trailheads in Arenas de San Pedro?
A: While specific trailhead parking information varies, Arenas de San Pedro and its surrounding villages generally offer parking options. It's recommended to check local maps or komoot tour details for designated parking areas near your chosen starting point.
tips:
Castle built in the 15th century. In the year 1393, King Henry III granted the title of Villa to Arenas de San Pedro, handing over its lordship to Ruy López Dávalos, constable of Castile, who began its construction.
It is known as the Castle of the Sad Countess, because after the execution of Don Álvaro de Luna in 1453, Juan II allowed the town to remain in the possession of his widow, Doña Juana de Pimentel, popularly known by this nickname.
Nice views when we ride on a dirt road near the town of El Arenal. It is an ideal area for mountain bike lovers, as it has countless trails to enjoy.

It is a charming town in the Sierra de Gredos, located south of the Los Galayos gorge. Perfect as a base for exploring these alpine landscapes, it is also worth a brief visit for its narrow, cobbled streets.
Viewpoint with impressive views of the Arbillas valley, from the Rosarito reservoir to Cabeza del Cervunal (2089 m). Don't hesitate to stop to appreciate these landscapes.

The Achecabos Bridge is commonly called a Roman bridge (since it was built on a previous bridge, predictably Roman). It is the oldest bridge in Arenas de San Pedro. It is expected that it was built in the eleventh century, so it is of medieval origin. It was widely used by travelers and transhumant shepherds, previously paying the toll or barge.
It consists of three arches, the central one being the largest of all. Its firm has been restored with cobblestone imitating the original. On both sides there is a one meter high stone parapet and various parts of its layout have been repaired with cement.

waypoints.name: Castle of the Constable Dávalos (Castle of the Sad Countess)
